description: Returns the current date/time as a Java Date object. Exact match, no arguments.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to the current date and time
constraints: No formatting option here — for a formatted string see B-NOW

description: Returns the local machine's hostname via InetAddress.getLocalHost().getHostName().
example_request: Include the server hostname in this parameter
constraints: Throws UnknownHostException if resolution fails; no fallback handling

description: Current epoch time in milliseconds, returned as a Java long.
example_request: Give me the current epoch millis as a number

description: Current epoch time in milliseconds, returned as a String instead of a long.
example_request: Give me the current epoch millis as a string

description: Reads <key> from the session_information map and returns the raw value.
example_request: Use the value of session variable customerAccountId
constraints: Evaluator-A only reads session_information; it cannot read DTO/JSON fields. For DTO JSON fields use Evaluator B/C plain JsonPath.

description: Casts a literal or a $S_<key> session lookup to Integer.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to the session value totalCount as a whole number
constraints: Only supports a bare literal or one level of $S_ nesting, not arbitrary nested expressions. If <rest> starts with $O_ it is not implemented (silently skipped).

description: Casts a literal or a $S_<key> session lookup to String.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to the session value channel as a string

description: Parses a literal true/false string into a Boolean.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to a fixed boolean true
constraints: Literal only — does NOT support $S_ nesting, unlike $INT_/$STR_/$DOU_ in the same evaluator.

description: Casts a literal or $S_<key> session lookup to Double. Defaults to 0 if session key is missing.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to the session value price as a decimal number

description: Casts a literal to Short.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to a fixed short value 3
constraints: Literal only, no $S_ nesting.

description: Parses a date string using the given format, either a literal value or a session-sourced value.
example_request: Parse this literal date string 01-07-2026 in dd-MM-yyyy format
constraints: Nested $ splitting is fragile; keep formats simple and prefer copying an existing working pattern from the codebase rather than composing a novel one.

description: Reformats a date. Supports a $S_ sub-form (reformat a session date) and a $DF_ sub-form (delegates to another getDynamicparamValue call to obtain the date first).
example_request: Reformat the session date deliveryDate from dd-MM-yyyy to dd/MM/yyyy
constraints: This is a DIFFERENT token shape from Evaluator B's $FORMATDATE_. Do not mix the two evaluators' syntax.

description: Shifts 'now' by the given Calendar field amounts (add operation). Multiple shifts separated by semicolons.
example_request: Give me a date 7 days from now
constraints: <field> must be the RAW INTEGER value of the java.util.Calendar constant (e.g. Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), not its name. This integer mapping is NOT defined inside Util.java — verify the correct constant value against your JDK / existing usages before using this token; do not guess the integer.

description: Sets specific Calendar fields on 'now' to a literal amount, or to that field's actual maximum/minimum value.
example_request: Set the date to the last day of the current month
constraints: Same caveat as A-RELDATE_: <field> is a raw Calendar integer constant, not verified/mapped in this file.

description: Combined SET/ADD Calendar field chain, optionally starting from a session-sourced base date instead of 'now'. ADD supports unit words (Hourly/Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Yearly) read from a session key instead of a literal field int.
example_request: Take the session date startDate and add 3 to Calendar field 5
trigger_phrases: change date based on another date
constraints: Base date (if using $S_ prefix) is parsed with the fixed format 'EEE MMM dd HH:mm:ss z yyyy' — the session value must already be in that exact format or parsing fails silently/throws.

description: Builds an array of Integer from a comma-separated literal list.
example_request: Give me a fixed array of integers 1, 2, 3

description: Builds an array of String from a comma-separated literal list.
example_request: Give me a fixed array of strings a, b, c

description: Concatenates the evaluated results of each semicolon-separated sub-expression. Each sub-expression is itself re-evaluated by getDynamicparamValue (recursive).
example_request: Combine session first name and last name with a space between them
constraints: This is the Evaluator-A CONCAT — syntax overlaps with Evaluator B's $CONCAT_ but each piece is re-evaluated by the respective evaluator's own rules.

description: Literal null value. Note: no leading $ sign, unlike every other token in this evaluator.
example_request: Set this mediation parameter to null
constraints: Must be exactly 'NULL' with no $ prefix — do not write $NULL.

description: Multiplies dash-separated numbers together. Each part is a literal number or $S_<key>. Result returned as a long-valued string.
example_request: Multiply the session quantity by 2.5
constraints: Only multiplication is supported here — for division see 'unsupported: division operator'.

description: JsonPath query against the workflow context object (param.getWorkflowContext()), serialized to JSON internally.
example_request: Read order.total from the workflow context object
constraints: Requires param.getWorkflowContext() to be non-null; NOT for DTO JSON — that is Evaluator B/C's job.

description: JsonPath query against the order-info context object (param.getContextOrderInfo()), serialized to JSON internally.
example_request: Read customer.id from the order-info context object
constraints: Requires param.getContextOrderInfo() to be non-null.

description: If nothing above matches, the raw attribute value is returned unchanged as a literal.
example_request: Just use this fixed text as-is

description: Static literal string, returned as-is (prefix stripped).
example_request: Set this DTO attribute to the fixed literal ACTIVE

description: Replaces the token '$SPACE_' with a literal space, keeping the remainder as text.
example_request: Insert a literal space character

description: Reads <key> from the session map and returns its toString(), or null.
example_request: Use the session value msisdn for this DTO field

description: Extracts only numeric characters (and a leading '-') from the nested evaluation result, discarding everything else.
example_request: Strip out everything except digits from this JSON field

description: Parses the nested evaluation result as Integer.
example_request: Cast the session value count to an integer for this DTO field

description: Extracts only alphabetic characters from the nested evaluation result, discarding everything else (digits, punctuation, etc).
example_request: Strip out everything except letters from this field

description: Adds 5% VAT to a numeric nested result and rounds to 2 decimal places using DecimalFormat('#.##').
example_request: Add 5% VAT to this price field
constraints: VAT percentage is HARD-CODED at 5% inside the Java method. There is no parameterized VAT-percentage token — for other percentages see $DEDUCTPER_ (deduction only, not addition) or note this is unsupported for custom add-percentages.

description: Deducts <percent>% from the nested numeric evaluation result, rounded to 2 decimals.
example_request: Give a 10% discount off this price
constraints: Splits internally on '_', so <rest> itself must not introduce ambiguous extra underscores at the split point — in practice this works because Java split() with limit defaults, but keep the value side (after the percent) as the next token, e.g. $.price not a raw underscore-heavy literal.

description: Concatenates the evaluated results of semicolon-separated sub-expressions, recursively evaluated by this same evaluator.
example_request: Combine a literal greeting with the session name

description: Casts the nested evaluation result to Long. If the original attributeValue had a trailing $M_<mapping> suffix, a mapping pass is applied to the result afterward via getMappedValue.
example_request: Convert this JSON field to a long number
constraints: Only exists in Evaluator B. Evaluator A has NO $LONG_ token — use $INT_ there if integer range suffices, otherwise flag as unsupported for Evaluator A.

description: Casts the nested evaluation result to Float.
example_request: Convert this field to a float number

description: Casts the nested evaluation result to Double.
example_request: Convert this field to a double / decimal number

description: Casts a literal to Short.
example_request: Set this field to a fixed short value 2
constraints: Literal only, no nesting/JsonPath support.

description: Casts the nested evaluation result to BigDecimal.
example_request: Convert this field to a BigDecimal money value

description: Converts <num> (evaluated) expressed in unit <uom> (evaluated; TB/GB/MB/KB) into raw bytes, via calculateDataInBytes.
example_request: Convert this data size field from GB to bytes
constraints: Supported source units: TB, GB, MB, KB (case-insensitive). Anything else falls through to the 'else' branch which treats the value as an already-raw number via Long.parseLong.

description: Converts <num> in unit <uom> (TB/GB/MB) into kilobytes, via calculateDataInKBytes.
example_request: Convert this data size field from MB to kilobytes
constraints: Supported source units for this token: TB, GB, MB only (no KB source case defined — a KB source falls to the else/raw-number branch).

description: Converts <num> in unit <uom> into seconds, via getValueInSec. ONE DIRECTION ONLY: source units MINS or DAYS convert TO seconds.
example_request: Convert this duration field from minutes to seconds
constraints: Supported source units: MINS, DAYS only. This token does NOT convert FROM seconds to anything else, and there is no companion token that converts seconds TO minutes — see 'unsupported: seconds to minutes conversion'.

description: Converts <num> in unit <uom> into hours, via getValueInHour. Source units: SECONDS, MINS, DAYS all convert TO hours.
example_request: Convert this duration field from minutes to hours
constraints: Supported source units: SECONDS, MINS, DAYS. This is the only unit-conversion token that accepts SECONDS as a source. It does NOT produce a minutes output — output unit is always hours.

description: Returns the string 'true' if ANY of the pipe-separated candidate sub-expressions evaluates to a non-null/non-empty value, else 'false'. Optional trailing #mapping (see mapping-syntax records) remaps the true/false result.
example_request: Check if either promoA or promoB has a value, return true/false
trigger_phrases: any of these has a value
check if any field is set

description: Returns the VALUE ITSELF of the first pipe-separated candidate that is non-null/non-empty (not true/false).
example_request: Use whichever of these fields actually has a value: work email or home email

description: Same logic as $ANYVALID_ but returns '1' (any valid) or '0' (none valid) instead of true/false strings.
example_request: Return 1 or 0 depending on whether either field has a value
constraints: Does NOT support a trailing #mapping suffix, unlike $ANYVALID_/$ANYTRUE_/$ALLTRUE_.

description: Returns '1' if ANY pipe-separated sub-expression evaluates (as Boolean) to true, else '0'. Optional #mapping remaps the 1/0 result.
example_request: If either flagA or flagB is true, show YES, otherwise NO

description: Returns '1' only if ALL pipe-separated sub-expressions are true, else '0'. Optional #mapping remaps the 1/0 result.
example_request: Only if both flagA and flagB are true

description: Builds a Java Map<String,Object> from evaluated key=value pairs, semicolon separated. Returns a Map OBJECT, not a string.
example_request: Build a map with id and name fields from the DTO
constraints: Downstream code consuming this attribute must expect a Map object, not a plain string field.

description: Parses the nested string result against a FIXED list of known formats (in order): 'MMM d, yyyy HH:mm:ss a', 'dd-MM-yyyy', 'dd/MM/yyyy', 'dd-MMM-yyyy'.
example_request: Parse this JSON date field into a Date object
constraints: Only these 4 formats are tried, in this order, first match wins. If the source format isn't one of these 4, parsing silently fails (returns null) — verify the source format matches one of the 4 before using this token.

description: Formats a Date object (produced by evaluating <rest>) into a string using pattern <fmt>.
example_request: Format the orderDate field as yyyy-MM-dd
constraints: <rest> MUST evaluate to an actual Date object (e.g. via $DATE_ or another date-producing token), not a raw string.

description: Reparses a date string using <oldFmt>, then reformats it to <newFmt>. Three '$'-separated parts.
example_request: Reformat orderDate from dd-MM-yyyy to yyyy/MM/dd
constraints: Different token shape from Evaluator A's $FORMATDATE_ — do not mix the two evaluators' syntax.

description: Returns the current date. If <fmt> is provided, returns a formatted string; if empty, returns a raw Date object.
example_request: Give me today's date formatted as yyyy-MM-dd

description: Casts the nested evaluation result to Boolean.
example_request: Convert this field to a boolean

description: Title-cases each whitespace-separated word of the nested string result (first letter uppercase, rest lowercase), via convertToCamelCase. Only applies if the nested result doesn't itself start with '$'.
example_request: Convert this name field to Title Case
constraints: Despite the name 'CAMEL', the actual output is Title Case (each word capitalized, space-separated) — NOT camelCase (no spaces, only first word lowercase). Do not assume camelCase output from this token name.

description: Raw JsonPath read against the DTO JSON. If the result is a single-element JSON array, it is unwrapped to just that element's string value; otherwise the full JSON representation is returned as a string.
example_request: Read items[0].sku directly via JsonPath

description: Raw JsonPath read (delegates to readJsonPath, no array-unwrapping logic applied).
example_request: Read the full items array via JsonPath

description: Default/fallback branch: reads the DTO JSON directly via JsonPath. A '+' between multiple paths ANDs their boolean-cast results together. A trailing '#mapping' applies a mapping table (see mapping-syntax record) to the read value.
example_request: Just read this field directly from the DTO JSON, no transform needed
constraints: VERIFIED IN CODE: this method has NO logic anywhere that strips or adds a leading '$.' -- after optionally splitting off '#mapping' and/or '+' combinators, whatever string remains is passed VERBATIM to readJsonPath()/JsonPath.read(). Unlike Evaluator C, there is no normalization step here.

Practical implication: whether '$.' is required depends entirely on what convention the calling system/config already uses when populating this attributeValue string, since Util.java itself does not add it. If DTO mapping configs are observed to work WITHOUT a leading '$.', that means either (a) something upstream of getCorrespondingAttributeValue prepends '$.' before this method runs, (b) those fields actually resolve through $JSONPATH_/$JSONARRAY_ instead of this fallback branch (both also pass the path verbatim), or (c) the JsonPath library version in use is lenient about a missing leading '$' -- this cannot be confirmed from Util.java alone.

Do not assume parity with Evaluator C's evalAttribute -- that method actively strips a leading '$.' before its own terminal JsonPath call, so behavior observed there does not prove anything about this method.

description: Complex-method token (detected via regex ^\$[^_]+_). String-replaces <search> with <replace> in the nested-evaluated result.
example_request: Replace dashes with slashes in this date field

description: Complex-method token. Splits the nested-evaluated result into a List<String> using <delimiter> as the split regex.
example_request: Split this comma-separated field into a list

description: Complex-method token. Performs arithmetic on the nested-evaluated numeric result. <op> must be one of '+', '-', '*'. <value> may itself start with '$' to be re-evaluated as an expression before use.
example_request: Add 10 to this base amount field
constraints: ONLY +, -, and * are implemented in the Java switch statement. There is NO '/' (division) case — passing '/' falls to default and silently returns '0'. Do not use $MATH for division or any operator outside +,-,*.

description: Complex-method token. Nested-evaluated result must be a List; returns the element at 0-based index <idx>, or null if out of range. Optional second quoted param applies a mapping pass (via doMapping) to the picked element.
example_request: Take the first item (index 0) from this list field

description: Complex-method token. Boolean XOR of the nested-evaluated result and <otherExpr> (re-evaluated if it starts with '$'). Optional second param applies a mapping pass to the boolean result.
example_request: Give me the exclusive-or of flagA and flagB

description: Looks up <key> in the 'contents' config map, then RE-EVALUATES that looked-up value as a new expression (recursive call to evalAttribute).
example_request: Look up defaultChannel in the config map and evaluate it
constraints: Only valid in Evaluator C (evalAttribute) — Evaluator A/B have no concept of a 'contents' config map.

token: $. (leading, i.e. value literally starts with the two characters '$.')
description: Strips a leading '$.' prefix and re-evaluates the remainder recursively. Largely an internal normalization step.
example_expression: (internal use — rarely authored directly by a human)
example_request: N/A — internal normalization, do not author this directly
constraints: This is an internal quirk of evalAttribute's recursive design, not a human-facing token. Prefer writing plain JsonPath expressions instead (see C-plain-jsonpath).

description: Static literal value.
example_request: Set this field to the fixed literal Approved

description: Reads the <n>th (0-based) pipe-separated value out of the paramValues input string passed into evalAttribute.
example_request: Use the first positional parameter passed in
constraints: Only valid in Evaluator C. paramValues is expected to be pipe ('|') delimited by the caller.

description: Default branch: JsonPath read against the DTO JSON. Array results are flattened to a comma-joined string. A trailing '#mapping' triggers evalIfElse (a richer mapping variant than getMappedValue — see mapping-syntax records for $BLANK and backslash-contains-match support).
syntax: $.some.path   OR   $.status#ACTIVE|1,ELSE|0   OR   status#ACTIVE|1,ELSE|0 (leading '$.' optional -- see constraints)
example_request: Map the plan code field to a single-letter code
constraints: VERIFIED IN CODE: evalAttribute() has an explicit branch 'else if (attributeValue.startsWith(JSON_PATH_TOKEN))' where JSON_PATH_TOKEN = "$." -- if the input starts with '$.', that prefix is stripped via replaceFirst and the method recurses on the bare remainder. The recursed call then falls through to the same default/else branch that a bare (no '$.') input would have hit directly.

Practical implication: a leading '$.' is FUNCTIONALLY INERT in evalAttribute. By the time the expression reaches the terminal evalJsonPath()/JsonPath.read() call, it never has a '$.' prefix on it -- this is true whether you originally wrote '$.subscriberType' (stripped then recursed) or 'subscriberType' (goes straight to the else branch). There is no code path in evalAttribute that re-adds '$.' before the real JsonPath call.
Do NOT assume this same leniency applies to Evaluator B (getCorrespondingAttributeValue) -- that method has no equivalent stripping logic; see B-plain-jsonpath.

Recommended human-facing convention: omit '$.' when authoring expressions specifically for evalAttribute/Evaluator C, since it adds a redundant recursion hop with no functional benefit. Keep '$.' when authoring for Evaluator B unless you have separately confirmed your config-loading layer already strips/adds it upstream.

description: General mapping-table suffix usable after $ANYVALID_/$ANYVALIDVAL_/$ANYTRUE_/$ALLTRUE_ (Evaluator B), plain JsonPath (Evaluator B/C), and getMappedValue callers. Comparison is case-insensitive exact match unless using the backslash-contains syntax (Evaluator C evalIfElse only).
example_expression: EVALUATOR B: $.subscriberType#PREPAID|1,POSTPAID|2,ELSE|0  (leading '$.' required -- verbatim pass-through to JsonPath, see B-plain-jsonpath)   |   EVALUATOR C: subscriberType#PREPAID|1,POSTPAID|2,ELSE|0  (leading '$.' optional/inert -- gets stripped before the real JsonPath call either way, see C-plain-jsonpath)
example_request: If subscriberType is PREPAID show 1, POSTPAID show 2, otherwise 0
trigger_phrases: if value equals X show Y
constraints: ELSE is a reserved catch-all key. The literal string 'null' can be used as a key to match a null value.

The presence/absence of a leading '$.' on the JsonPath part (before the '#') is EVALUATOR-DEPENDENT -- do not give one universal example. See B-plain-jsonpath and C-plain-jsonpath constraints, and NUANCE-dollar-dot-prefix, before answering a mapping query. Always confirm which evaluator (B or C) processes the target field first.

description: If a matched mapping result starts with $EVAL_, the remainder is re-evaluated as a fresh expression against the same JSON/session context, instead of being returned as a plain literal.
example_request: If the flag is true, compute the discount framework field instead of a static value
trigger_phrases: compute a different field based on condition
constraints: Only meaningful inside a mapping table's result slot — do not use $EVAL_ as a top-level standalone expression.

description: If a matched mapping entry has no pipe/result part, the entire mapping call returns null.
example_request: N/A - unusual/edge-case authoring, avoid unless intentionally returning null on match
constraints: Rarely an intentional human request — flag this shape as likely a typo (missing '|<result>') rather than assuming it's deliberate.

description: Special mapping key meaning 'the evaluated value is blank or null'. Only supported inside evalIfElse (Evaluator C), NOT in getMappedValue (Evaluator B).
example_request: If the middle name field is empty or missing, show N/A
trigger_phrases: if field is empty show default
constraints: This special key is exclusive to Evaluator C's mapping (evalIfElse). Using $BLANK as a key inside an Evaluator B mapping (getMappedValue) has no special meaning there.

description: A mapping key wrapped in backslashes means 'does this key list contain the evaluated value as a substring' rather than requiring exact equality. Only supported in evalIfElse (Evaluator C).
example_request: If the error message contains the word TIMEOUT anywhere, show Retry
constraints: Exclusive to Evaluator C. Evaluator B's getMappedValue has no contains-match capability — only exact case-insensitive equality.

token: $. (leading prefix on a plain JsonPath expression)
description: Whether a plain JsonPath expression needs a leading '$.' is EVALUATOR-DEPENDENT and was confirmed by direct code trace (not by general JsonPath-library assumptions). EVALUATOR B (getCorrespondingAttributeValue): the default/else branch (~line 2897) does NOT strip or add '$.' anywhere -- after optionally splitting off '#mapping' and/or '+' combinators, the attributeValue string is passed VERBATIM into readJsonPath()/JsonPath.read(). Whatever convention is used at the call site is exactly what JsonPath receives. EVALUATOR C (evalAttribute): there is an explicit branch -- 'else if (attributeValue.startsWith(JSON_PATH_TOKEN))' where JSON_PATH_TOKEN = "$." -- that strips a leading '$.' via replaceFirst and recurses. The recursed call (now without '$.') falls into the same default/else branch that a bare, no-'$.' input would hit directly on the first pass. By the time the expression reaches the terminal evalJsonPath()/JsonPath.read() call, it NEVER has a leading '$.' on it, regardless of whether the original author included one. There is no code path in evalAttribute that re-adds '$.' before the real JsonPath call.
syntax: N/A -- this is a behavioral note, not a token to author directly
example_request: Should I write $.subscriberType or just subscriberType for this DTO field?
trigger_phrases: should I include the dollar dot
constraints: For Evaluator C (evalAttribute): '$.' is functionally inert/optional -- omit it, since including it only adds a redundant recursion hop with identical end behavior.

For Evaluator B (getCorrespondingAttributeValue): '$.' presence is NOT normalized by Util.java at all. Whether it's required depends on upstream conventions this file cannot confirm (e.g. a config loader that may or may not prepend it, or whether the field actually resolves through $JSONPATH_/$JSONARRAY_ instead of the plain fallback -- both of which also pass paths verbatim with no stripping).

If you have empirically observed working Evaluator-B DTO configs that omit '$.', trust that empirical observation over any generic JsonPath-library assumption -- but note Util.java itself does not explain that behavior, so the explanation lies outside this file (upstream config layer, wrapper method, or library version behavior).
Always identify which evaluator (B or C) is actually processing the target expression BEFORE answering a '$.' question -- giving one blanket answer across both evaluators is incorrect.

description: There is NO division operator anywhere in this DSL. $MATH (Evaluator B) only implements '+', '-', '*' in its Java switch statement; passing '/' silently falls to default and returns '0' (not an error, a wrong silent result). $MULTI_ (Evaluator A) only multiplies.
correct_response_guidance: State explicitly that division is not supported by any existing token. Do not use $MATH("/",...) — it silently returns 0, not an error, which is a dangerous trap. Recommend either doing the division upstream before the value enters this DSL, or extending Util.calculate() to add a '/' case before using this token.

description: There is no token that converts a SECONDS value into MINUTES. $SEC_ only converts MINS/DAYS -> seconds (not the reverse). $HOUR_ converts SECONDS/MINS/DAYS -> hours (not minutes). No $MIN_ token exists anywhere in Util.java.
correct_response_guidance: Do not combine $SEC_ or $HOUR_ to fake this, and do not invent a $MIN_ token. State that seconds-to-minutes conversion is not implemented in this DSL. Legitimate options: perform the division upstream before the value reaches this expression layer, or extend Util.java (e.g. add a getValueInMin helper and a $MIN_ token, mirroring getValueInHour) before using it here.

description: Evaluator A (getDynamicparamValue, mediation parameters) has no $LONG_ token. $LONG_ only exists in Evaluator B (getCorrespondingAttributeValue, DTO fields).
correct_response_guidance: For a mediation parameter (Evaluator A context), the closest supported option is $INT_ (Integer range only). If true 64-bit long precision is required for a session-sourced mediation parameter, flag this as unsupported and suggest routing the value through Evaluator B/DTO instead, or extending Evaluator A with a $LONG_ case mirroring Evaluator B's.

description: $VATABLE_ always adds a hard-coded 5% — there is no parameter to specify a different VAT/tax percentage with this token.
correct_response_guidance: For any percentage other than a straight 5% addition, $VATABLE_ cannot be used. $DEDUCTPER_<percent>_ only performs a DEDUCTION, not an addition. There is no generic 'add X percent' token. Recommend using $MATH("*","1.10")_<expr> style multiplication as the closest workaround for a fixed custom percentage addition (e.g. 10% => multiply by 1.10), and note this is a workaround, not a purpose-built token.

description: $ANYVALIDB_ does not support a trailing #mapping suffix, unlike its siblings $ANYVALID_, $ANYTRUE_, $ALLTRUE_.
correct_response_guidance: If a mapped/remapped output is needed, use $ANYVALID_ (true/false + #mapping) instead of $ANYVALIDB_ (1/0, no mapping support).

description: $BLANK and backslash-wrapped contains-match mapping keys are Evaluator-C-only features (evalIfElse). They do NOT work inside Evaluator B's getMappedValue-based mappings (e.g. after $ANYVALID_, $ANYTRUE_, plain JsonPath in Evaluator B).
trigger_phrases: blank check in Evaluator B mapping
correct_response_guidance: If the target field belongs to Evaluator B, do not use $BLANK or backslash-contains syntax — these only exist in Evaluator C. Evaluator B only supports exact case-insensitive key matching plus ELSE and the literal 'null' key.

description: Tokens are NOT interchangeable across evaluators even when they look similar (e.g. Evaluator A's $FORMATDATE_ vs Evaluator B's $FORMATDATE_ have different argument shapes; Evaluator B's $BYTE_/$SEC_/$MATH/$INDEX/etc. do not exist in Evaluator A; Evaluator C's $C./PARAM_ do not exist in A or B).
correct_response_guidance: Always confirm which evaluator (A: mediation parameter / B: DTO attribute / C: JSON-config-driven attribute) the target expression will run through BEFORE picking a token. If uncertain, ask, or state the assumption explicitly and flag it as an assumption.
